Subsea Study Guide Day 3 Questions: What factors might cause pump pressure at kill rate to exceed the calculated Initial Circulating Pressure during kill startup procedures?
(A): The close circulating rate pressure will be different because the hole is deeper since it was recorded.
(B): The mud weight has increased due to contamination from the formation.
(C): The pump efficiency has decreased causing higher required pressures.
(D): The choke line friction has increased due to temperature effects.

Subsea Study Guide Day 3 Questions: What method for measuring Choke Line Friction (CLF) will result in the lowest Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD)?
(A): Circulating down the choke line and up the Riser.
(B): Circulating down the drill pipe and up the choke line.
(C): Increasing the pump rate to reduce friction losses.
(D): Using heavier mud to overcome friction effects.

Subsea Study Guide Day 3 Questions: How does static mud in deepwater subsea choke and kill lines potentially affect shut-in pressure measurements?
(A): The static mud will cause surface pressures to read higher than actual.
(B): The higher gel strength at depth may cause the surface casing pressure to be lower than it should be.
(C): The gel strength will have no effect on pressure readings at surface.
(D): The static mud will cause drill pipe pressure to increase significantly.

Subsea Study Guide Day 3 Questions: After completing the first circulation of the Driller's Method, the Shut-in Casing Pressure (SICP) is 300 psi higher than the original Shut-in Drillpipe Pressure (SIDPP). What is the appropriate response?
(A): Increase the mud weight immediately to balance the higher pressure.
(B): Shut in the well and wait for pressures to stabilize before continuing.
(C): Resume circulation and continue until all the influx is out of the hole and SICP and Shut-in Drillpipe Pressure (SIDPP) are equal.
(D): Begin the second circulation phase of the Driller's Method immediately.
